title: TIP-1.0 Pak Reason Codes — Recall observability registry
description: Catalogue of reason-code values that may be attached to a Pak via the recall storage join table `pak_reason_codes`. Codes are observability tags surfacing why a Pak was selected; they do NOT drive ranking on the OSS side. OSS persistence + inspection is transparent; scoring against these codes is Pro daemon behaviour. New OSS-side codes require a specification amendment. Pro-only extensions are admitted via a sibling `pak-reason-codes-v1.pro.json` overlay and MUST NOT be required by OSS receivers. See https://docs.tokenpak.ai/protocol/multipak-recall.

version.description: Registry-document schema version. Bumps follow additive-within-TIP-1.x semantics; only a specification amendment may add codes.

codes.description: Ordered catalogue of reason codes. Order is informational; receivers MUST treat the set as unordered.

code.description: Snake_case identifier. Lowercase + digits + underscore only.

category.description: Grouping per the reason-code table. `exclude` codes indicate the Pak was rejected from the package — they are observability, not enforcement.

description.description: Human-readable description of when this code applies.

codes.description: The current task explicitly references this Pak.
Marked as required by caller or governance.
Carries acceptance criteria for the current task.
Caller explicitly pinned.
Pak's topic/project matches the requesting agent's role.
Pak relates to the current task type (implement/review/plan/etc.).
Pak's anchors reference a file present in the task scope.
Pak's anchors reference a test file in the task scope.
Pak references a dependency of the task scope.
Pak relates to the current workflow type.
Pak's updated_at is within the recency window.
Pak links to a recent failure event.
Pak references a ratified standard that applies.
Pak is a governance pin or invariant.
Pak surfaces a risk relevant to the task.
Pak captures a prior accepted resolution.
A compressed/summarized form is preferred over raw.
Raw content exceeds the budget; consider compression or deferral.
Pak is deferred but hydratable on request.
Pak content is stable across sessions; cache-friendly.
Pak is reusable across tasks within a project.
Pak is a candidate for cache-stability ordering.
Match score below threshold.
Pak's domain does not match the task.
Pak content superseded or aged out.
Pak duplicates another included Pak (content-hash match).
Pak would exceed the package token budget.
Pak hits a policy gate (sensitive list, scope, permission).
